title: React Frontend Specialist Mock Test
description: Comprehensive assessment for frontend specialists covering architecture, hooks, state management, performance, and modern React 18 features.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between the 'Render Phase' and the 'Commit Phase' in React?
options: Render is synchronous, Commit is asynchronous
Render identifies changes (can be paused), Commit applies changes to the DOM (cannot be paused)
Render phase is where effects run, Commit phase is for logic
There is no difference in React 18
questions.explanation: In Concurrent React, the render phase is a pure calculation phase that React can interrupt. The commit phase is where React actually touches the DOM.

questions.text: Which hook is specifically designed to prevent 'tearing' in concurrent rendering when using external stores?
options: useEffect
useLayoutEffect
useSyncExternalStore
useCallback
questions.explanation: useSyncExternalStore is the recommended hook for subscribing to external data sources to ensure consistency across concurrent updates.

questions.text: What is the result of using 'className' instead of 'class' in JSX?
options: It applies CSS classes to the rendered DOM element
It defines a JavaScript class for the component
It is ignored by the browser
It throws a syntax error in modern React
questions.explanation: JSX uses 'className' because 'class' is a reserved keyword in JavaScript.

questions.text: How can you optimize a component that re-renders frequently with the same props?
options: Wrap it in React.memo()
Use the useMemo hook
Use the useRef hook
Move state to localStorage
questions.explanation: React.memo is a Higher Order Component that memoizes a component and skips re-rendering if props are shallowly equal.

questions.text: What does the 'key' prop do in a list of elements?
options: It provides a unique ID for CSS targeting
It helps React identify which items have changed, been added, or removed for efficient DOM updates
It sets the index of the array automatically
It binds the element to a database record
questions.explanation: Keys are essential for React's reconciliation process to track stable identities of list items.

questions.text: When using `useEffect`, what happens if you return a function?
options: The function executes on every render
The function is used for state updates
The function acts as a cleanup mechanism (runs before unmount or next effect run)
It stops the effect from ever running again
questions.explanation: Returning a function from useEffect is the standard pattern for cleaning up subscriptions, timers, or event listeners.

questions.text: What is the purpose of `useId` in React 18?
options: To generate random keys for map() functions
To generate unique, stable IDs for accessibility attributes that match between server and client
To identify the current user session
To track the component's depth in the tree
questions.explanation: useId provides stable IDs for ARIA attributes, preventing hydration mismatches in SSR apps.

questions.text: How do you define a 'Fragment' in React?
options: <Fragment />
<React.Fragment> ... </React.Fragment>
Both B and C
questions.explanation: Fragments can be defined using either the long form or the shorthand syntax.

questions.text: Which hook should be used to store a mutable value that doesn't trigger a re-render when changed?
options: useState
useRef
useMemo
useReducer
questions.explanation: useRef returns a plain JavaScript object that persists for the lifetime of the component and doesn't trigger updates when modified.

questions.text: What is 'Lifting State Up'?
options: Moving state to a global Redux store
Moving state to the closest common ancestor to share data between sibling components
Using a Higher Order Component to manage state
Updating the parent's state from a child component
questions.explanation: It is the standard pattern for sharing data between components that do not have a direct parent-child relationship.

questions.text: In React 18, what does `startTransition` do?
options: It marks a state update as a non-urgent transition
It forces an immediate re-render
It creates a CSS animation for the component
It initializes the routing state
questions.explanation: Transitions allow React to prioritize urgent updates (like typing) over non-urgent ones (like rendering a list).

questions.text: What is the 'Virtual DOM'?
options: A physical copy of the browser DOM
A lightweight representation of the UI kept in memory and synced with the 'real' DOM
A tool for debugging styles
An alternative to the window object
questions.explanation: React uses the Virtual DOM to calculate the minimum set of changes required to update the UI.

questions.text: Which hook is most appropriate for complex state logic that involves multiple sub-values?
options: useState
useReducer
useContext
useEffect
questions.explanation: useReducer is preferred over useState when state transitions are complex or depend on the previous state.

questions.text: What is a 'Portal' in React?
options: A way to fetch data from external APIs
A method to render children into a DOM node that exists outside the hierarchy of the parent component
A secure way to store API keys
An entry point for a micro-frontend
questions.explanation: Portals are commonly used for modals, tooltips, and floating menus.

questions.text: Which lifecycle method in class components is equivalent to `useEffect(() => {}, [])`?
options: componentDidUpdate
componentDidMount
componentWillUnmount
render
questions.explanation: An empty dependency array in useEffect makes it run once after the initial mount, similar to componentDidMount.

questions.text: What is 'Hydration' in React?
options: Adding data to the cache
Attaching event listeners to server-rendered HTML on the client side
Cleaning up memory leaks
The process of loading CSS before JS
questions.explanation: Hydration turns static HTML from the server into an interactive application on the client.

questions.text: What occurs during 'Tearing' in concurrent rendering?
options: The screen flickers white
Different components show different values for the same state during a single render pass
The application crashes with a stack overflow
Styles fail to load correctly
questions.explanation: Tearing is an inconsistency where the UI shows 'multiple versions' of the same data simultaneously.

questions.text: How does `useDeferredValue` improve performance?
options: It delays the execution of a function by a fixed time
It lets you defer re-rendering a non-urgent part of the tree until after more urgent updates
It memoizes a value indefinitely
It fetches data in the background
questions.explanation: It is similar to debouncing but integrated into React's scheduling to prioritize user interaction.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `forwardRef` function?
options: To pass state to a child component
To allow a component to pass a ref it receives to a child DOM element
To create a reference to a functional component
To improve the performance of list rendering
questions.explanation: forwardRef is necessary when a parent needs access to a DOM node inside a child functional component.

questions.text: Which tool is commonly used for 'Component Virtualization' in React?
options: React Router
react-window / react-virtualized
Redux
Axios
questions.explanation: Virtualization libraries render only the visible items in a massive list, significantly improving performance.

questions.text: What is the 'Compound Component' pattern?
options: Combining two React apps into one
Components that work together to share state implicitly (e.g., Select and Option)
A component that uses multiple hooks
A component that renders another component
questions.explanation: Compound components provide a declarative API where sub-components share logic via Context.

questions.text: In React 18, what is the default behavior of 'Batching'?
options: It only batches updates in React event handlers
It batches all state updates together, including inside Promises and timeouts
It disables batching for better debugging
It requires a manual flag to enable
questions.explanation: React 18 introduced Automatic Batching for all updates, reducing the number of re-renders.

questions.text: Which hook is used to perform side effects that require DOM measurements synchronously?
options: useEffect
useLayoutEffect
useInsertionEffect
useMemo
questions.explanation: useLayoutEffect fires synchronously after all DOM mutations, making it safe for measuring layout.

questions.text: What is a 'Controlled Component' in React forms?
options: A component managed by the browser's native form handling
A component where the form data is handled by a React component's state
A component that cannot be edited by the user
A component that uses external libraries like Formik
questions.explanation: In controlled components, the 'source of truth' for the input value is the React state.

questions.text: How do you provide a value to a Context at the top of a component tree?
options: <MyContext.Consumer value={...} />
<MyContext.Provider value={...} />
<MyContext value={...} />
useContext(MyContext, value)
questions.explanation: The Provider component is used to wrap the subtree and provide the context value.

questions.text: What does `React.lazy` allow you to do?
options: Delay a state update
Load components dynamically (Code Splitting)
Optimize the Virtual DOM comparison
Cache API responses
questions.explanation: React.lazy helps reduce the initial bundle size by loading components only when they are needed.

questions.text: What is 'Tree Shaking' in a React project?
options: Removing old components from the folder
A build-time process that removes unused code from the final bundle
Reorganizing the folder structure for speed
Optimizing CSS selectors
questions.explanation: Modern bundlers use static analysis to exclude code that is never imported.

questions.text: Which metric measures the visual stability of a page in React applications?
options: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)
CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)
FID (First Input Delay)
TTFB (Time to First Byte)
questions.explanation: CLS measures how much elements move around as the page loads, which is a key Core Web Vital.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'Suspense' component?
options: To handle errors in components
To show a fallback UI while children are loading (e.g., lazy components or data)
To pause a component's execution
To speed up state updates
questions.explanation: Suspense provides a declarative way to handle loading states for asynchronous work.

questions.text: What does the `useCallback` hook return?
options: The result of the function
A memoized version of the function that only changes if dependencies change
A promise that resolves to a function
Nothing (it is a side effect hook)
questions.explanation: useCallback is used to maintain referential identity of functions passed as props to optimized child components.
